failure_modes.text: Teams can treat a bad payoff structure as if better individual behavior alone will rescue the outcome.
failure_modes.source_quote: The PD highlights a crucial insight: sometimes a strategic interaction is governed by "irredeemably lousy rules".
failure_modes.mitigation: Before assuming betrayal dynamics, check the payoff structure: verify that the situation is actually a prisoner's dilemma rather than a coordination problem solvable through process design.

failure_modes.text: People can impose betrayal logic on a coordination problem that is actually better explained by process repair or shared identity.
failure_modes.source_quote: Anti-Pattern: Treating Every Coordination Problem as Betrayal.
failure_modes.mitigation: Explore mechanism design: ask whether changing the rules, communication channels, or enforcement structures could shift the game to one where cooperation is the dominant strategy.

failure_modes.text: A competitive frame can become self-fulfilling when ambiguous moves are read as proof that the other side will defect.
failure_modes.source_quote: we might misinterpret a competitor's action ... which reinforces our own doubts and pressures us toward the herd action (betrayal)
failure_modes.mitigation: Test ambiguous moves charitably before defaulting to defection: when the other side's intent is unclear, design a low-cost probe that reveals intent without triggering escalation.

premortem_questions.text: What frame other than a competitive one could govern this interaction?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What frame other than a competitive one could govern this interaction?

premortem_questions.text: What would you have to believe to accept the competitive outcome as unavoidable?
premortem_questions.source_quote: What would you have to believe?

premortem_questions.text: What trust, enforcement, or repeat-interaction mechanism would have to exist for cooperation to survive?
premortem_questions.source_quote: Works when trust, enforcement, or repeat interaction are the real design variables.

heuristics.text: Make the payoff structure explicit before blaming individual actors for a bad collective outcome.
heuristics.source_quote: The model helps separate bad actors from bad rules by making the payoff structure explicit.

heuristics.text: Simulate likely countermoves so the team reasons from incentives instead of from one-sided intention stories.
heuristics.source_quote: Teams may be broken into an "attack team" and a "defense team," playing out a series of moves and countermoves.

heuristics.text: Change the game when possible by improving cheating detection, punishment, or collaboration conditions instead of optimizing inside a broken rule set.
heuristics.source_quote: The most critical actionable step derived from the PD is the effort to change the game itself.

curation_notes.summary: The source is naturally chunk-ready on bad rules, competitive framing, and mechanism design for cooperation.
donor_drops: Did not preserve donor game-theory generalities beyond the raw source's clearer payoff-structure, frame, and trust-design material.
open_questions: The third failure mode compresses the source's cascade and doubt-amplification material so it stays reusable beyond classic rival-firm cases.

allies: The raw ally section is strong on systems-thinking and logic-tree style decomposition for later relation preservation.
antagonists: The raw antagonist section contains cascade and principal-agent pressure, but only current-vocabulary targets should survive Wave 3.
structured_tensions: The source contains an explicit structured tension block against active-listening, empathy, and confirmation-bias.
